steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The phrase reads: "a photograph by British [___] Chris Killip." The blank supplies the word "artist" plus optional punctuation. When a proper name immediately follows a descriptive noun phrase to identify who that noun refers to, no punctuation is needed — the name and the noun form a single identifying unit.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'artist;': a semicolon joins two independent clauses — "British artist" is not an independent clause, so this is incorrect. ✗
  • Choice B — 'artist—': a dash here creates an abrupt break that implies a list or interruption, fragmenting the smooth noun phrase. ✗
  • Choice C — 'artist': no punctuation — "British artist Chris Killip" flows as one unified noun phrase identifying the photographer. ✓
  • Choice D — 'artist,': a comma after a restrictive appositive is incorrect; the name "Chris Killip" is essential to identify which artist, so no comma separates it. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
